perturbation.original_text: Company shall have forty-eight (48) hours (exclusive of Saturday, Sunday and holidays) from receipt of the notice to elect, by written notice, whether it will participate in accordance with Section 2.3. BP shall not Complete the Initial Well until Company has notified BP in writing whether or not it will participate or until forty-eight (48) hours (exclusive of Saturday, Sunday and holidays) have elapsed since Company's receipt of BP's notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall have twelve (12) hours from receipt of the notice to elect, by written notice, whether it will participate in accordance with Section 2.3. BP shall Complete the Initial Well as soon as possible, no matter Company has notified BP in writing whether or not it will participate or until twelve (12) hours have elapsed since Company's receipt of BP's notice.
perturbation.explanation: Decreasing the election period to 12 hours (from 48 exclusive of weekends/holidays) and forcing the completion of the well no matter the response of the company. This potentially violates regulations about providing reasonable time for decision-making in significant investments.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Louisiana Revised Statutes 30:103.1 regarding notification and objection periods for well operations.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly setting timelines in all cases, Louisiana law prioritizes fair and transparent operations in oil and gas activities. R.S. 30:103.1 requires operators to consider the rights and interests of all parties, which is potentially violated by forcing a decision within 12 hours. I couldn't find direct timeline, so this is a weaker contradiction.

perturbation.justification: The original contract provided 48 hours (exclusive of weekends/holidays) for the company to decide on participation, whereas the changed text reduces this to 12 hours and forces completion of the well regardless of the company's decision. The law explanation states that Louisiana law prioritizes fair and transparent operations in oil and gas activities, and that R.S. 30:103.1 requires operators to consider the rights and interests of all parties. This is potentially violated by forcing a decision within 12 hours, creating a contradiction because the changed text undermines the consideration of all parties' rights by imposing an unreasonably short decision-making timeframe. The scraped law snippet 2, although incomplete, suggests that terms and conditions apply to the service, reinforcing the concept that there is an expected standard that has been superseded. This falls under the definition of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' because the time-sensitive obligations in the changed contract do not align with the legal requirement of fair and transparent operations and considering the rights of all parties.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party may change its address by notifying the other Party in writing of such address change, and the change will be effective thirty (30) days after such notification is received by the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party may change its address by notifying the other Party in writing of such address change, and the change will be effective five (5) days after such notification is received by the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification period for address changes to 5 days from 30. This can create issues with legal notices and compliance deadlines, potentially contradicting due process if five days is insufficient for mail delivery.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Louisiana Code of Civil Procedure Article 1314 mandates a reasonable timeframe for service and notification in legal proceedings.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1314 requires reasonable time to locate the correct agent for service. 5 days may be insufficient time to update records and ensure proper legal service, especially if the initial notification arrives to the old address.
perturbation.justification: The change from 30 days to 5 days for address change notification creates a potential contradiction, as explained in the Perturbation and Law explanations. The explanations indicates Article 1314 requires reasonable time to locate the correct agent for service, and that 5 days may be insufficient. The Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition states that a contradiction arises when a contract sets a deadline that violates state law, making the terms unenforceable. While no scraped law snippet directly mentions a timeframe for address changes, the general principle of 'reasonable time' is understood in legal context to allow proper legal service, which this change arguably impedes.

perturbation.original_text: BP shall then have an optional prior right, for a period of fifteen (15) days after receipt of such written notice, to purchase for the stated consideration on the same terms and conditions the interest which Company proposes to sell.
perturbation.changed_text: BP shall then have an optional prior right, for a period of two (2) days after receipt of such written notice, to purchase for the stated consideration on the same terms and conditions the interest which Company proposes to sell.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the preferential right to purchase period to 2 days (from 15). This is unduly short and potentially violates principles of good faith and fair dealing implicit in Louisiana contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Louisiana Civil Code Article 1983 concerning good faith performance of contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1983 mandates good faith. Two days is highly unreasonable for a party to perform due diligence. Even if the text does not explicitly require the parties to perform due diligence or consult any expert, that the timeframe is so short is arguably acting in bad faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the time BP has to exercise its right to purchase from 15 days to 2 days. The Perturbation Explanation asserts this is unduly short. The Law Explanation states this timeframe might violate good faith principles under Article 1983. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines good faith as encompassing 'honest dealing' and the 'observance of fair dealing standards'. Reducing the period to 2 days can be viewed as a failure to observe fair dealing standards and thus a breach of good faith, creating a legal contradiction as defined under Inconsistencies.
